result.markdown:
--- title: "R Orientation" subtitle: "Getting comfortable with R and RStudio — just enough to follow along" author: "R for HTA (Basics) — Workshop 2026" institute: "RRC-HTA, AIIMS Bhopal | HTAIn, DHR" format: revealjs: theme: [default, ../../custom-theme.scss] slide-number: true footer: "R for HTA (Basics) — RRC-HTA, AIIMS Bhopal" logo: "" transition: fade width: 1280 height: 720 code-fold: true code-overflow: wrap highlight-style: github fig-align: center execute: echo: true eval: false warning: false message: false --- ## What This Session Is (and Isn't) This is **not** an R programming course. . . . You do not need to memorise anything here. . . . **Goal:** Build enough familiarity that when you see R code: - You can recognise what it is doing - You can change an input value - You can run it and see output - You are not intimidated by it . . . Think of this as learning to **read a language** well enough to follow a conversation — not to write poetry. ## The RStudio Interface ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(DiagrammeR) grViz(" digraph rstudio { graph [bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=11] source [label='SOURCE EDITOR\n(Write & edit code)\n.R and .qmd files', fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white', width=3] env [label='ENVIRONMENT\n(Variables in memory)\nData you have created', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white', width=3] console [label='CONSOLE\n(Run code here)\nSee text output', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white', width=3] output [label='FILES / PLOTS / HELP\n(See visualisations)\nBrowse files', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white', width=3] source -> console [style=invis] env -> output [style=invis] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-rstudio-layout .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` The four panels of RStudio ::: ::: ## Running Your First Code ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # This is a comment — R ignores everything after # message("Welcome to R for HTA!") ``` ::: If you see the message, your R installation is working. ## Variables and Assignment In R, you store values using `<-` : ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Store some values drug_cost <- 5000 # Cost of the drug in ₹ admin_cost <- 800 # Administration cost in ₹ total_cost <- drug_cost + admin_cost # Print the result total_cost ``` ::: When you see this: **a value is stored, and you can change that number.** ## Vectors: Collections of Values A vector is a collection of values. This is how R handles data efficiently: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Cost of five different treatments treatment_costs <- c(1200, 3400, 5600, 2300, 4100) # Operations on vectors mean(treatment_costs) # Average sum(treatment_costs) # Total min(treatment_costs) # Minimum max(treatment_costs) # Maximum ``` ::: In HTA modelling: transition probabilities, costs per cycle, utility values — all vectors. ## Data Frames: Tables in R If you are used to Excel spreadsheets, a **data frame** is the R equivalent: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create a data frame — like an Excel table interventions <- data.frame( strategy = c("Standard Care", "New Screening", "Enhanced"), cost = c(1200, 2500, 4800), qalys = c(0.78, 0.85, 0.91) ) interventions ``` ::: ## Accessing Data Frames ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Access the cost column interventions$cost # Calculate mean cost mean(interventions$cost) ``` ::: The `$` symbol means "get this column from that data frame". ## Plotting with ggplot2 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot(interventions, aes(x = qalys, y = cost, label = strategy)) + geom_point(size = 4, colour = "#4e79a7") + geom_text(vjust = -1.2, size = 3.5) + labs(x = "QALYs", y = "Cost (₹)", title = "Cost vs QALYs for Three Interventions") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: ggplot is your best friend for visualisations. ## Installing vs Loading Packages R packages are like add-ons: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Install a package (only need to do this once) install.packages("ggplot2") # Load a package (need to do this each session) library(ggplot2) ``` ::: **Analogy:** Installing is like downloading an app. Loading is like opening it. ## Pattern 1: Defining Model Parameters ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Transition probabilities (annual) p_mild_to_moderate <- 0.15 p_moderate_to_severe <- 0.10 # Costs in ₹ c_mild <- 5000 c_moderate <- 25000 ``` ::: When you see this: **these are the model inputs — change these numbers to explore scenarios.** ## Pattern 2: A Loop Over Cycles ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} n_cycles <- 10 annual_cost <- 5000 discount_rate <- 0.03 total_cost <- 0 for (cycle in 1:n_cycles) { discounted <- annual_cost / (1 + discount_rate)^cycle total_cost <- total_cost + discounted } cat("Total discounted cost:", round(total_cost, 0), "\n") ``` ::: When you see a `for` loop: **the model repeats a calculation across time cycles.** ## Pattern 3: Creating a Plot ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) # Prepare data cycle_data <- data.frame( cycle = 1:10, discounted_cost = annual_cost / (1 + discount_rate)^(1:10) ) # Create plot ggplot(cycle_data, aes(x = cycle, y = discounted_cost)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + labs(x = "Year", y = "Discounted Cost (₹)", title = "Effect of Discounting") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: When you see `ggplot`: **a chart is being created.** ## The Read → Modify → Run → Interpret Workflow ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} grViz(" digraph workflow { graph [rankdir=LR, bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled,rounded', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=12] A [label='READ\nthe code\n(understand logic)', fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white'] B [label='MODIFY\na parameter\n(change a number)', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white'] C [label='RUN\nthe chunk\n(click play)', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white'] D [label='INTERPRET\nthe output\n(what changed?)', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white'] A -> B -> C -> D D -> B [style=dashed, label='Try again', fontsize=9] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-workflow .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` The workshop workflow ::: ::: ## Working with .qmd Files All workshop materials are in **Quarto (.qmd)** files: - **Text** — explanations - **Code chunks** — grey blocks with R code - **Outputs** — results, tables, plots . . . **To work with a .qmd file:** 1. Open it in RStudio 2. Read the text explanations 3. Run code chunks one at a time (click the green play button ▶) 4. See the output appear below each chunk 5. Modify values and re-run ## Three Things to Remember 1. **Values are stored with `<-`** - Change them by editing the number . . . 2. **Code runs top to bottom** - Run chunks in order . . . 3. **If something breaks, read the error message** - It often tells you what went wrong Everything else you can look up — or ask an AI tool. ## Try It Yourself ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # YOUR TASK: Change these values and re-run cost_A <- 3000 cost_B <- 1500 qaly_A <- 0.82 qaly_B <- 0.75 # Calculate ICER icer <- (cost_A - cost_B) / (qaly_A - qaly_B) cat("ICER = ₹", round(icer, 0), "per QALY gained\n") # Is it cost-effective? wtp <- 100000 if (icer < wtp) { cat("→ Cost-effective at WTP = ₹", format(wtp, big.mark=","), "\n") } else { cat("→ NOT cost-effective\n") } ``` ::: If you ran this and changed the result — **you are ready for the rest of the workshop.**

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
